scenario: External service calls Operate REST API. Team wonders OAuth scopes -- restrict to read-only.
question: Operate REST API auth scopes?

options.text: Camunda 8 uses a resource-permission authorization model that controls access at a fine-grained level. Authorization grants specify an owner (user, group, role, or mapping), a permission type (READ, CREATE, UPDATE, CANCEL, DELETE), and a resource. For an external service needing read-only access, granting only READ_PROCESS_DEFINITION and READ_PROCESS_INSTANCE permissions restricts it from performing write operations. The model enforces access control at the Orchestration Cluster level via the Admin component. API calls without the required permissions receive a 403 Forbidden response.

Custom code -- this overstates what is needed. Restricting an external service to read-only Operate API access does not require custom authorization code. The Camunda 8 authorization framework handles this through configuration in the Admin component: create an API client with a specific identity, configure authorizations granting only READ_* permissions on the relevant resources, and the platform enforces these restrictions automatically. Custom middleware or proxy code for permission filtering would duplicate what the platform already provides natively.

md:36-103: the authorization model grants specific permissions (READ_PROCESS_DEFINITION, READ_PROCESS_INSTANCE, CREATE_PROCESS_INSTANCE, UPDATE_PROCESS_INSTANCE, CANCEL_PROCESS_INSTANCE, DELETE_PROCESS_INSTANCE) on resources to owners (users, groups, roles, or mapping rules). For a read-only external service, configure an authorization granting only READ_PROCESS_DEFINITION and READ_PROCESS_INSTANCE on the relevant process definitions with a wildcard. The bearer token from OAuth identifies the caller; the Admin component checks resource permissions and returns 403 Forbidden for unauthorized actions. Default deny: nothing is accessible unless explicitly granted.

a.text: Incorrect. Operate API scopes: OAuth scopes: Granularity: Resource + action: process-instances:read. Per API resource: separate scopes. Read vs write vs admin: levels. Example permissions per authorizations.md:84-103 table (Resource → Permission): Process Definition → READ_PROCESS_DEFINITION / READ_PROCESS_INSTANCE Process Definition → CREATE_PROCESS_INSTANCE / CANCEL_PROCESS_INSTANCE (write) Decision Definition → READ_DECISION_DEFINITION / READ_DECISION_INSTANCE Batch → CREATE_BATCH_OPERATION_RESOLVE_INCIDENT (incident resolve) Resource → CREATE (deploy -- equivalent to RCE per :151) / READ Audit Log → READ Client configuration: In Console (SaaS): Create client: pick scopes. Minimal: only what client needs. In Identity (Self-Managed): Configure client roles + scopes. Keycloak realm: define. For the scenario (external service read-only): Create client with only :read scopes. No write permissions. Even if client compromised: limited damage. Server enforcement: Each API call: token includes scopes. Server checks: scope sufficient? If not: 403 Forbidden. For "all reads": Multiple :read scopes: combine. Or wildcard: :read (verify support). For audit clients: Read-only: standard. Audit log access: special scope. For ops clients: Need write: resolve incidents, cancel instances. Wider scope. For developer clients: Possibly write to dev cluster. Read-only to prod. For service-to-service: Each service: own client. Scoped per service needs. Principle of least privilege: Why: Compromise mitigation: smaller blast radius. Accidental damage prevention. Audit clarity: who can do what. Implementation: Default deny: explicit allow. Review periodically: scope creep. Token claims: OAuth token includes scopes as claim. Server validates on each request. For multi-tenant: Scopes per tenant. No cross-tenant even with valid scope. For rotation: New token: same scopes typically. Update scopes: regenerate token. For testing: Test scopes: verify rejection of unauthorized. Smoke tests: per-scope test. For SaaS: Console manages clients. Scope selection: at client creation. For Self-Managed: Identity / Keycloak: realm configuration. Client roles: equivalent to scopes. For Tasklist API: Similar scoping: tasks:read, tasks:write. For Zeebe gRPC: Different model: typically broader auth. Per-cluster auth. For Camunda 8 maturity: Scope granularity evolving. Verify per version. Best practices: Least privilege: minimum scopes. Review access: periodic. Rotate credentials: regular. Audit scope usage: identify excess. Test scope enforcement. Anti-patterns: Admin scope for all clients: over-permissioned. No scope review: drift over time. Sharing credentials: lose audit. For the scenario (external read-only): ` Owner: external-monitoring (Mapping Rule for OIDC client) Authorizations on Process Definition (): READ_PROCESS_DEFINITION READ_PROCESS_INSTANCE No CANCEL_/UPDATE_/MODIFY_/DELETE_ permissions. Reads succeed; mutating calls return 403 Forbidden per orchestration-cluster-api-rest-authentication.md:61`. For monitoring tools: Read-only sufficient: just query metrics. No need for write. For automation tools: Limited write: e.g., resolve incidents but not deploy. Scoped narrowly.
